questions.questionText: Relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD/Marcus Gunn pupil) is:
questions.optionB: Paradoxical dilation of the affected pupil when light is swung from the normal eye to the affected eye (indicates optic nerve/retinal damage on the affected side)
questions.explanation: Core concept: RAPD = optic nerve disease → affected pupil dilates when light swung to it (swinging flashlight test). Why correct: In optic nerve disease (optic neuritis, CRAO, traumatic optic neuropathy), the affected eye perceives less light. Swinging flashlight: when light moves from normal → affected eye → brain perceives dimming → both pupils dilate. Why A wrong: Anisocoria = unequal pupils (different from RAPD — RAPD can have equal pupil sizes at rest). Why C wrong: Fixed dilated pupil → CN III palsy (efferent pathway). Why D wrong: Normal pupil constricts to light; RAPD paradoxically dilates. Clinical relevance: RAPD indicates AFFERENT pathway damage (retina or optic nerve). NOT seen in: cataract (media opacity), refractive errors, macular disease (usually). Exam trap: RAPD is never caused by cataract (even dense cataract — enough light gets through). If RAPD present over cataract → suspect retinal detachment or optic nerve disease. Memory aid: 'RAPD = Response Absent (Paradoxical Dilation) = optic nerve problem.' Reference: Khurana – Neuro-ophthalmology.
questions.conceptTag: RAPD Marcus Gunn afferent optic nerve swinging flashlight
